section.title: Testing the Spirits
section.sectionRationale: Having spoken of the Spirit God has given, John warns that not every spirit is from God. He gives the test that separates the Spirit of God from the spirit of antichrist: the confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh. Believers, indwelt by the greater One, have already overcome the false prophets.
section.thesis: Because many false prophets have gone out, believers must test the spirits by the confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, which marks the Spirit of God against the spirit of antichrist, and they can do so with confidence because the One in them is greater than the one in the world.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the call to test the spirits; the confession of Christ come in the flesh as the test; and the assurance that believers, from God, have overcome those who are from the world.

units.label: Test the spirits
units.note: many false prophets have gone out

verses.text: Beloved, do not believe every {{pneuma|spirit}}, but {{dokimazo|test}} the spirits to see whether they are from God. For many false prophets have gone out into the world.

units.label: The test: Christ come in the flesh
units.note: the Spirit of God against the antichrist

verses.text: By this you will know the Spirit of God: Every spirit that {{homologeo|confesses}} that Jesus Christ has come in the {{sarx|flesh}} is from God,
and every spirit that does not confess Jesus is not from God. This is the spirit of the antichrist, which you have heard is coming and which is already in the world at this time.

units.label: You have overcome them
units.note: greater is He who is in you

verses.text: You, little children, are from God and have {{nikao|overcome}} them, because greater is He who is in you than he who is in the world.
They are of the world. That is why they speak from the world's perspective, and the world listens to them.
We are from God. Whoever knows God listens to us; whoever is not from God does not listen to us. That is how we know the Spirit of truth and the spirit of {{plane|deception}}.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. Here it refers to the spiritual power or influence behind a teacher or prophet, whether the Holy Spirit or a deceiving spirit. Because unseen powers stand behind human messages, John says not every spirit is to be believed; each must be tested.

dokimazo.gloss: To test, examine, prove, as metal is assayed for genuineness. Believers are to weigh the spirits behind teaching rather than accept every claim. The test is not suspicion for its own sake but discernment aimed at recognising what is truly from God.

homologeo.gloss: To confess, acknowledge, say the same thing openly. The decisive test is the open confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh. This is not bare words but genuine acknowledgment of the incarnate Son; the Spirit of God leads people to own this truth, while deceiving spirits deny it.

sarx.gloss: Flesh, the physical human nature. To confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is to affirm the real incarnation, that the Son truly took a human body. It directly counters teaching that the divine Christ only seemed to be human, striking at the heart of the gospel.

nikao.gloss: To conquer, overcome, prevail. John tells the believers they have already overcome the false prophets, not by cleverness but because the One in them is greater. The victory is grounded in God's indwelling presence, giving confidence to discern and resist error.

plane.gloss: Error, deception, wandering. The spirit of deception, or of error, leads people astray from the truth about Christ. It is set against the Spirit of truth; the two are told apart by whether they confess or deny the incarnate Son and whether they heed the apostolic word.

basic.context: John has just spoken of the Spirit God gives, so now he warns: not every spiritual message is from God. Many false prophets are out there. So test the spirits. The test is clear and about Jesus: does the message confess that Jesus Christ truly came in the flesh, as a real human? If so, it is from the Spirit of God. If it denies Him, it is the spirit of antichrist. Believers need not panic, because the God who lives in them is greater than any power in the world, and they have already overcome the deceivers.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the classic passage on discernment. The test is doctrinal at its core: the truth about who Jesus is, God the Son come in real flesh. John is not encouraging endless suspicion but giving a sure measure. The confession is not mere lip service; it is genuine acknowledgment of the incarnate Son, which the false teachers refused. Assurance and discernment go together: believers can test boldly because the greater One indwells them.
basic.originalLanguages: Do not believe every spirit, pneuma, but test, dokimazo, them. The mark of the Spirit of God is that a spirit confesses, homologeo, that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, sarx. Believers have overcome, nikao, the false prophets because the greater One is in them, and so they can tell the Spirit of truth from the spirit of deception, plane.
basic.typology: The many false prophets going out fulfil the pattern of Israel's false prophets, who spoke their own words while claiming God's authority. The Spirit of truth answers the promised Spirit poured out in the last days, given to lead God's people into truth.
basic.christ: The whole test is Christ: Jesus Christ come in the flesh, truly God and truly man. The incarnation is the dividing line between the Spirit of God and the spirit of antichrist. To confess the real, enfleshed Son is to be from God; to deny Him is to be from the world.
basic.god: God gives His Spirit of truth and indwells His people, so that the greater One in them overpowers the spirit at work in the world. He does not leave His children defenceless against deception but equips them to discern and to overcome, grounding their confidence in His own presence.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 13 and 18 test prophets by their message and fruit. Matthew 7 warns of false prophets known by their fruit. John 16:13 promises the Spirit of truth, and 1 John 2 and 2 John 7 give the same test of Christ come in the flesh.
basic.oneStory: God has always had to guard His people from false prophets who speak lies in His name. As Messiah's age dawns, the test sharpens to the person of Christ Himself. God gives His Spirit of truth to lead His people, and the confession of Jesus come in the flesh becomes the line between truth and deception in the story's final act.

context.literary: The section flows from the mention of the Spirit at the end of chapter 3 and sets up the great treatment of love that follows. It is built on paired contrasts: the Spirit of God against the spirit of antichrist, those from God against those from the world, the Spirit of truth against the spirit of deception.
context.historical: Itinerant teachers claiming prophetic inspiration moved among the churches, and the secessionists denied that the Son had truly come in the flesh. John gives a concrete, teachable test so ordinary believers could distinguish true from false messengers rather than being swayed by spiritual claims.
context.canonical: The passage stands in the biblical tradition of testing prophets, Deuteronomy 13, 18, Matthew 7, now focused christologically. It matches the test of confession in 1 John 2:22-23 and 2 John 7, and rests on the promise of the Spirit of truth in John's Gospel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn believers that not every spiritual message is from God, to give them a reliable test centred on the confession of Jesus Christ come in the flesh, and to assure them that, indwelt by the greater One, they have already overcome the false prophets.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verse 1 prescribes the action, test the spirits, and verses 2 and 3 give the test. Verses 4 to 6 describe the settled realities: believers are from God and have overcome, the false teachers are from the world, and the two spirits are told apart by response to the truth.

debates.question: Is the test of a true spirit purely doctrinal, or does it include life and conduct?

views.label: The confession of Christ come in the flesh is the decisive test, inseparable from a life shaped by the truth
views.case: John makes the christological confession the explicit criterion here: whether a spirit confesses Jesus Christ come in the flesh. Yet across the letter this confession is never mere words; it is joined to obedience and love, which are the fruit of the Spirit of truth. So the doctrinal test is primary and decisive, while genuine confession shows itself in a life that heeds the apostolic word, verse 6. This integrated reading is strongest and fits the whole letter.

views.label: The test is only about correct doctrine
views.case: Some isolate the confession as a purely intellectual checklist; but John elsewhere warns that even claiming to know God while disobeying is a lie, so bare doctrine alone is not John's whole concern.

views.label: The test is mainly about conduct, with doctrine secondary
views.case: Others stress fruit and love so heavily that the confession of Christ recedes; but here John makes the confession of the incarnate Son the explicit dividing line.

debates.bearing: The strongest orthodox reading keeps the confession of Jesus Christ come in the flesh as the decisive, primary test, while recognising that a true confession is never bare words but is borne out in obedience, love, and heeding the apostolic message. Doctrine and life are distinguished but not divided.

study.originalLanguages: John warns against believing every pneuma, every spirit, the unseen power behind a message, and calls believers to dokimazo, to test and assay them like metal proved for genuineness. The single decisive test is whether a spirit will homologeo, openly confess, that Jesus Christ has come in the sarx, in real human flesh, affirming the true incarnation against those who said the Son only seemed to be human. Believers have already nikao, overcome, the false prophets, not by their own strength but because the greater One dwells in them. So they can tell apart the Spirit of truth and the spirit of plane, of deception and error, that leads people to wander from Christ.

typology.type: The many false prophets gone out into the world
typology.fulfillment: The false prophets who plagued Israel, speaking their own words in God's name, now intensified in the last hour and tested by the confession of Christ, Deuteronomy 18:20-22.

typology.type: The Spirit of truth given to God's people
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit promised for the last days and given by Christ to guide His people into all truth, John 16:13, enabling them to discern and overcome.

study.christ: Christ Himself is the test. The confession that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, truly God and truly man, is the line between the Spirit of God and the spirit of antichrist. Any spirit that denies the real incarnation of the Son is not from God, for it strikes at the heart of the gospel of the God who came to save. To own the enfleshed Christ is the surest sign that the Spirit of truth is at work.
study.god: God does not abandon His people to a confusing swirl of spiritual voices. He gives His Spirit of truth and dwells within them, so that the One in them is greater than the one in the world. He equips ordinary believers to discern by a clear test and assures them they have already overcome the deceivers. He is a God who guards His children's minds as well as their hearts.

crossReferences.note: A test for prophets who presume to speak in God's name, the Old Testament root of testing the spirits.
Beware of false prophets; you will know them by their fruits, a complementary test to John's confession of Christ.
The Spirit of truth guides believers into all truth, the Spirit John contrasts with the spirit of deception.
Many deceivers deny that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh; this is the deceiver and the antichrist, the same test as here.

study.oneStory: God has always had to protect His people from prophets who lie in His name. As the age of Messiah dawns, the test comes to a single point, the person of Christ come in the flesh. God pours out His Spirit of truth to lead His people and equips them to discern, so that the confession of the incarnate Son becomes the dividing line between truth and deception in the closing chapters of the story. Those in whom the greater One dwells overcome the deceivers and hold fast to the real Jesus.

sermon.title: Testing the Spirits
sermon.why: Begg preaches this passage, 1 John 4:1-6, on the call to test the spirits, the confession of Christ come in the flesh, and the assurance that the greater One indwells believers. A clear, orthodox handling of discernment.

quiz.q: Why does John tell believers not to believe every spirit?

options.text: Because many false prophets have gone out into the world.
options.note: Verse 1: the presence of many false prophets is why the spirits must be tested.

options.text: Because all spiritual experiences are meaningless.
options.note: John does not dismiss the spiritual realm; he calls for discernment within it.

options.text: Because believers can never know the truth.
options.note: John gives a clear test precisely so believers can know the Spirit of truth.

options.text: Because only trained leaders can discern anything.
options.note: John addresses all the little children, not only leaders; discernment is for the whole church.

quiz.q: What is the test John gives for the Spirit of God?

options.text: Whether a spirit confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh.
options.note: Verse 2 makes the confession of the real incarnation the decisive test.

options.text: Whether a teacher is impressive and popular.
options.note: The world listens to false teachers; popularity is no test.

options.text: Whether a message makes people feel good.
options.note: Feelings are no measure; the test is the confession of Christ come in the flesh.

options.text: Whether a teacher performs signs and wonders.
options.note: Signs can deceive; the sure test John gives is the confession of the incarnate Son.

quiz.q: Why can believers face false prophets with confidence?

options.text: Because greater is He who is in them than he who is in the world.
options.note: Verse 4 grounds confidence in the indwelling of the greater One.

options.text: Because they are naturally smarter than deceivers.
options.note: Confidence rests on God in them, not on native intelligence.

options.text: Because false teaching poses no real danger.
options.note: John takes the danger seriously; that is why he gives the test.

options.text: Because they have never encountered error.
options.note: They have overcome the deceivers, not avoided them; the victory is in the greater One within.

quiz.q: Is John's test of the spirits only about correct doctrine?

options.text: The confession of Christ is decisive, and true confession shows itself in heeding the apostolic word and in love.
options.note: Doctrine is primary here, yet inseparable from a life shaped by the truth across the letter.

options.text: It is purely an intellectual checklist with no bearing on life.
options.note: The confession of Christ truly is the decisive test, but John never treats it as bare words divorced from obedience and love.

options.text: It is only about conduct, and doctrine does not matter.
options.note: Here John makes the confession of the incarnate Son the explicit dividing line.

options.text: It has nothing to do with who Jesus is.
options.note: The test is precisely about who Jesus is, God the Son come in the flesh.

questions: John tells us to test the spirits rather than believe every message. What voices or teachers are shaping you right now, and how might you weigh them against the truth about Christ?
The test centres on Jesus Christ come in the flesh. Why is the real humanity of Jesus, alongside His deity, so essential to the gospel you trust?
Greater is He who is in you than he who is in the world. How does the indwelling presence of God steady you when you feel outnumbered or pressured by falsehood?
The world listens to those who speak from its perspective. Where are you tempted to trim the truth about Jesus to gain a hearing, and what would faithfulness look like instead?
Discernment and assurance go together in this passage. How can you grow in testing what you hear without slipping into either gullibility or constant suspicion?

translationNotes.note: 'Confesses that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh' affirms the real incarnation; the perfect tense stresses the abiding reality of the Son having come and remaining in the flesh.
'Does not confess Jesus' follows a shorter reading; the BSB footnote notes some texts read 'does not confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh'. The sense of the test is unchanged.
'The Spirit of truth and the spirit of deception' can also read 'the spirit of error'; the contrast is between the Holy Spirit's leading and the influence that makes people wander from Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 John 4:1-6. No em dashes in this passage. Variant at 4:3 noted per the BSB footnote.
